data.title: MID: Growth ETF With An ESG Approach
data.description:
MID strategy

American Century Mid Cap Growth Impact ETF (NYSEARCA:MID) is an actively managed ETF launched on 07/13/2020 with an objective of long-term capital growth. It has a portfolio of 40 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 0.15% and an expense ratio of 0.45%.

As described in the prospectus by American Century, eligible companies must align with at least one of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDG), meaning the fund has a loose environmental, social, and governance (“ESG”) approach. Then, portfolio managers select

…companies with attractive returns on invested capital that are demonstrating or expected to demonstrate long-term business improvement. Analytical indicators helping to identify or forecast signs of business improvement could include accelerating earnings or revenue growth rates, increasing cash flows, or other indications of the relative strength of a company’s business.

Like for most active ETFs, the strategy description offers a lot of flexibility to the fund’s managers and little transparency to investors. Additionally, MID doesn’t disclose its real holdings on a daily basis, but publishes a proxy portfolio supposed to track daily returns. The fund’s turnover rate was 68% in the most recent fiscal year. MID takes the Russell Midcap® Growth Index as a benchmark, but this article will use as a primary benchmark the S&P Mid-Cap 400 index, represented by iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH).

Portfolio

MID is almost exclusively invested in US companies (96% of asset value), with a focus on information technology (28%) and significant exposure in consumer discretionary (20%), industrials (19%) and healthcare (15%). Other sectors weigh no more than 8%. Compared to the benchmark, MID mostly overweights technology, consumer discretionary and healthcare, while it significantly downplays financials, consumer staples and ignores real estate and communication.

The portfolio is quite concentrated, but risks related to individual companies are moderate. Indeed, the top 10 holdings of the proxy portfolio, listed in the next table, represent 39% of asset value and the heaviest position weighs 4.12%.

Takeaway

American Century Mid Cap Growth Impact ETF (MID) is an active growth fund implementing a loose ESG approach. MID is diversified in 40 mid-size companies and has strong growth metrics. Despite good results over the last 12 months and beating its “ESG” competitor NUMG, MID is still unconvincing for now, with average performance, high volatility, and above-par fees.

data.title: Gravity's Global Rollout Turns A Single Franchise Into A Deep Value Play
data.description:
Intro

Gravity’s (NASDAQ:GRVY) first-quarter print showed that the Ragnarok engine is still humming. Revenue rose to KRW 137.5 billion, or US$93.2 million, up 6% sequentially and 14.8% year-on-year as two new mobile launches offset normal aging in legacy titles. Operating profit advanced 55% quarter on quarter to KRW 24.7 billion, lifting margin back above 18% despite a heavier royalty mix, while net profit of KRW 22.0 billion translated to roughly US$2.15 of quarterly EPS on the tiny 6.95 million-share base. The beat matters because the stock still trades on single-digit earnings, barely above net cash that already covers more than 90% of the share price. With a refreshed pipeline rolling out across three continents and a balance sheet that looks more like a bank than a game studio, Gravity offers rare asymmetry: investors are paying little more than cash for an IP platform that keeps compounding.

Thesis

Gravity’s long-term runway rests on the flywheel of Ragnarok IP, a portfolio that continually refreshes itself across PC, mobile and now console, then franchises outward into animation and consumer products. Management’s co-development and licensing model lets the company scale in markets from Southeast Asia to Latin America without large fixed costs, so incremental launches drop quickly to the bottom line. New pipeline entries such as Ragnarok Origin 2, AI-infused casual titles and blockchain experiments extend the brand to fresh demographics, while Gravity’s publishing arm adds a second growth lever by distributing third-party hits. The combination of net cash, no debt, and the strategic umbrella of majority parent GungHo positions Gravity to compound earnings as a lean, IP-rich platform in the global games' economy. When that strategy meets a valuation of 7–8 times trailing earnings and 0.4-times EBITDA, the bull case writes itself.

Ragnarok Flywheel

What keeps Ragnarok relevant two decades after launch is an always-on schedule of spin-offs tuned to distinct regional tastes. Q1’s lift was driven by Ragnarok M: Classic in Southeast Asia and Ragnarok Idle Adventure Plus in the global English market. Each title reuses core art and lore but adapts mechanics and monetization, allowing Gravity to amortize R&D across multiple SKUs. Because most development is outsourced to specialist studios, Gravity’s in-house team remains small, holding SG&A to 15.9% of sales last quarter even with marketing for fresh launches. High-margin licensing revenue from China and Japan further juices returns: royalty streams carry 80%-plus gross margin, so every new territory turns the flywheel faster.

A second spoke is brand extension. The company opened its first “Ragnarok Golf Monsters” indoor screen-golf venue in Taipei during Q1, testing whether mascot characters can convert into experiential retail. Early foot-traffic data are anecdotal, yet the concept underscores how Gravity thinks about lifetime customer value: deepen emotional engagement, then monetize outside the core game loop. If the experiment scales, consumer-products revenue, now just 2% of the mix, could grow without diluting digital focus.

Pipeline Momentum

Looking ahead, at least nine Ragnarok titles are slated for multi-region release over the next twelve months, including Ragnarok X: Next Generation for Western consoles, Ragnarok Crush (puzzle tower-defense) and Ragnarok Landverse, a blockchain PC entry targeting Latin America. The cadence matters because mobile game cohorts typically decay 30-40% a year; constant new drops keep aggregate revenue flat to up despite individual drags. Management’s guidance, embedded in the investor deck, calls for mid-teens top-line growth for full-year 2025, a figure now de-risked by the Q1 beat.

Crucially, Gravity is diversifying beyond its own IP. Q1 saw console releases of Twilight Monk and Snow Brothers 2 Special, plus the Japanese launch of JLPGA Heroine Collection. Third-party publishing gives Gravity a wider funnel for content while capping downside through revenue-share deals. If only two out of ten external titles hit, the model still clears cost of capital given a 30% royalty take and near-zero inventory risk.

Global Publishing Leverage

Gravity’s subsidiary network now spans 17 offices, the latest being Gravity Game Unite in Malaysia, opened in March. These micro-hubs localize events, community management and payment rails without duplicating headquarters functions, lowering effective customer-acquisition cost. The strategy shines in emerging markets where distribution can be fragmented: direct service of Ragnarok Online in Latin America kicks off this quarter, replacing a third-party operator and instantly improving margin capture.

Parent company GungHo, which owns roughly 59% of Gravity, adds another layer of leverage by sharing technology and IP pipelines. That backing reduces financing risk and provides bargaining power when negotiating with platform holders from Apple to Tencent. For minority investors, the structure is a mixed blessing, but it does ensure strategic patience and access to GungHo’s Japan user base, historically one of Ragnarok’s most profitable regions.

Financials

Revenue of KRW 137.5 billion translated into a 36.4% gross margin, flat year-on-year despite higher platform fees, thanks to the shift from paid advertising to community-driven virality after launch windows close. Operating expenses fell 22.9% sequentially as Q4’s marketing blitz for THE RAGNAROK normalized, lifting operating income back to 18% of sales. Net income margin of 16% still trails the 20% levels seen in pandemic-era peaks, yet the trajectory is improving.

Cash and short-term instruments totaled KRW 577.2 billion (US$391.4 million) at quarter-end, with no debt. That equates to roughly US$56 per ADR, covering 90% of today’s US$62.38 share price. Enterprise value is therefore just US$34 million, or 0.09 times trailing revenue and 0.45 times EBITDA. Operating cash flow has exceeded net income in five of the past six years, and capex runs below US$2 million annually, so free cash conversion stays north of 85%.

Management does not provide explicit quarterly guidance, but its May presentation reiterates an objective of “stable double-digit” revenue growth for 2025 driven by nine scheduled launches and geographic expansion. The Q1 run-rate already annualizes to US$373 million, implying that guidance is achievable with only low-single-digit sequential growth in the remaining quarters.

Risks

Ragnarok concentration remains the biggest swing factor. While multiple genres cushion the blow, any material decline in core loyalty could pressure margins because brand licensing fees are largely fixed. A second vector is platform risk: Gravity earns 85% of revenue from Android and iOS stores, so policy changes around IDFA or third-party billing could raise commission expense.

Regulatory exposure is meaningful. Titles slated for China releases require ISBN approval, and Beijing’s unpredictable licensing cadence can delay monetization. Although Ragnarok Landverse’s blockchain angle targets the crypto-friendly RONIN platform, a global backlash against play-to-earn could stall uptake. Currency is another watch-out: 75% of costs are in Korean won while 60% of revenue originates in US dollars or Thai baht, so a sharp KRW appreciation would compress gross margin. Finally, GungHo’s majority stake means minority holders have limited influence over dividends or buybacks, a concern highlighted in prior activist letters.

Conclusion

At US$62 per share, Gravity trades at 7.6 times trailing earnings, a PEG of 0.61, just 0.45 times EV/EBITDA and 1.18 times sales. NetEase (NTES), which also marries first-party development with publishing, changes hands at 19 times earnings, 13.9 times EV/EBITDA and 5.7 times sales. Roblox (RBLX), a purer UGC platform but a helpful benchmark for mobile engagement, fetches a 17.98 times sales multiple despite negative earnings. Even if Gravity merely rerates halfway toward NetEase on EV/EBITDA, the implied target is US$125 per share, double current levels, before assigning any option value to AI or blockchain initiatives.

The market’s skepticism stems from single-franchise dependence and governance optics, yet the numbers tell a different story. Ragnarok’s flywheel keeps spinning, the pipeline is broader than ever, and a net-cash balance equal to 90% of equity provides a built-in margin of safety. With high-teens ROE, consistent free cash generation and a valuation that prices in permanent decay, Gravity offers a classic mispricing. I remain firmly bullish and expect the stock to outperform global gaming peers as new releases roll through the model and investors rediscover the compounding power of an IP-rich platform bought at cash-box prices.

data.title: Bruker: Weakness In Academic Research Shadows Near-Term Growth; Initiate With 'Hold'
data.description:
Bruker (NASDAQ:BRKR) manufacturers instruments to detect and measure the structures of chemical, biological and industrial material samples. Its products include magnetic resonance spectrometers and mass spectrometry-based and molecular spectrometry-based systems. The company has grown its business via organic investments as well as acquisitions over the past few years. The weakness in academic research and tariff uncertainties are expected to pose significant growth challenges for Bruker in FY25. I am initiating with a ‘Hold’ rating with a fair value of $43 per share.

As shown in the table below, Bruker has delivered an average of 6.9% organic revenue growth over the past 8 years, with the only negative growth year during the global pandemic.

One of the key reasons for robust organic growth is its investments in key growth areas such as spatial biology, single-cell biology, and advanced microscopy, as presented in the slide below. Bruker’s products are used in the cell biology research, including both spatial and single-cell biology areas. I believe the rapid of adoption of spatial biology is driven by its ability to map molecules that traditional methods cannot capture. DeciBio predicts the spatial biology market will grow at a CAGR of 19% from 2025 to 2030. As such, Bruker’s unique product solutions could potentially sustain its rapid growth.

Beyond organic growth, Bruker has leveraged its balance sheet to pursue acquisitions. As detailed in the table below, the company made several acquisitions in FY24 to strengthen its spatial biology, molecular diagnostics as well as workflow automation markets.

Specifically, the company announced to acquire NanoString Technologies for approximately $392.6 million on April 22, 2024. NanoString’s products are used for spatial transcriptomics and gene expression analysis, and I think the deal will further strengthen Bruker’s portfolio in the spatial biology market.

In May 2024, the company completed its acquisition of ELITech, a fast-growing manufacturer of systems and assays for molecular diagnostics, for €870 million in cash. Notably, ELITech is a highly recurring business, with more than 80% of sales derived from consumables.

In April 2025, the company announced the acquisition of RECIPE, a Europe-based provider of therapeutic drug monitoring diagnostic kits. As detailed in the slide below, RECIPE could significantly strengthen Bruker’s capabilities in the small molecule diagnostic market. Overall, I favor Bruker’s acquisition strategy to broaden its market presence and solution portfolios.

As of Q1 FY25, Bruker had $184 million in cash and cash equivalents, and $2.1 billion in debt, resulting in a 4.5x net debt leverage ratio. I acknowledge its debt level is a bit high and its balance sheet is highly leveraged. During the earnings call, the management noted the company will continue to pay down its debt. On the positive side, the company has generated a total of $876 million in cash from operations over the past three years, while spending $351 million in CAPEX during the same period. As such, the company has the capability to lower its debt leverage over time.

Bruker released its Q1 FY25 result on May 7, 2025, posting 2.9% organic revenue growth and 1% adj. operating profit growth, a business deceleration from its previous quarter, as detailed in the chart below:

The key takeaway from the quarter was its growth slowdown driven by weak academic market and tariff dynamics. In other words, FY25 would be a transitional year with lower growth for the company. During the call, the management noted that the U.S. policy changes have caused a significant funding issue for academic research. In addition, the current tariff situation has brought growth challenges in the market demands within the pharma and biotech industries.

Bruker is guiding for 0%-2% organic revenue growth and 5%-8% constant EPS growth for FY25, as detailed in the slide below.

I agree with the management’s near-term view on end-market demands. I anticipate its revenue will grow by 5.5% in FY25, comprising 2% organic revenue growth, 1% growth from FX and 2.5% growth from acquisitions. The growth assumptions reflect the reduced funding in US academic research and the $100 million revenue impact due to tariffs as estimated by the management.

From FY26 onwards, I anticipate its organic revenue will recover to 6%, slightly lower than its historical average over the past eight years, as its revenue today almost doubled compared to 8 years ago. In addition, I assume the company will continue its acquisition strategy, allocating 8% of revenue to acquisitions, which I estimate could contribute an additional 2.5% growth to the topline.

I forecast its operating margin will expand by 50bps annually from FY26 onwards, driven by 20bps from pricing increase, 20bps from reduction in SG&A expenses and 10bps from revenue mix towards reagents and consumables. I estimate that total operating expenses will grow by 7.9% annually, leading to a 10.5% operating margin by FY34.

The DCF can be summarized as follows:

The FCFF can be summarized as follows:

The WACC is calculated to be 9.9% assuming: risk free 4.2%; beta 1.3; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $6.2 bn; debt $2.1 bn; tax rate 27.5%. I set the terminal growth rate to be 4%, aligning with the overall healthcare market growth.

Discounting all the future FCFF and adjusting net debt, the fair value is calculated to be $43 per share, as per my estimate.

Downside Risks

As discussed previously, the company carries $2.1 billion in debts on its balance sheet, a quite high level compared to its $6.2 bn market capitalization. I understand its high level of debts is largely caused by their past acquisitions, and the management plans to deleverage its balance sheet. I think investors need to monitor its debt repayment going forward, although I do not anticipate any liquidity issues at this moment.

As Bruker continues to use acquisitions to strengthen its portfolio and enter some new sub-markets, the company might face strong competitions from large established players like Danaher (DHR) and Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO). Compared to these companies, Bruker is a quite small player with limited capital and talent resources.

Lastly, China represented 14% of total revenue in FY24, declining by more than 10% year-over-year. Bruker’s business in China has been impacted by the weak economy and challenging funding environment in the pharma and biotech industries. It is unlikely for the company to turnaround its China business in the near term, in my view.

Conclusion

Bruker is likely to experience a lower growth in FY25 caused by tariff uncertainties and weakness in the academic research market. Over the long run, I believe its strategy in spatial biology and acquisitions could continue to fuel its business growth. I am initiating with a ‘Hold’ rating with a fair value of $43 per share.

data.title: Crown Castle Remains In Play
data.description:
A few months ago, in March 2025, I made the painful decision to downgrade telecommunications company Crown Castle (NYSE:CCI). This move came after management announced the sale of certain key assets, specifically of its Small Cells and Fiber Solutions operations, in a deal valued at $8.5 billion. This transaction, which was actually two transactions, would permit the company to reduce debt by $6 billion and to launch a $3 billion share buyback program. Even though the debt reduction was certainly welcomed, there had been rumors leading up to that point that the transaction could have been worth as much as $15 billion. Though most of the estimates figured it would be between $8 billion and $10 billion.

Since downgrading it from a ‘strong buy’ to a ‘buy’ in response to that development, shares of the company have generated a slight loss for investors of 0.1%. That comes at a time when the S&P 500 is up 8.3%. Looking at the picture today, I still feel every bit the same as I did back then. I remain disappointed by the proceeds that the company is getting from this deal. However, shares of the business are still priced at levels that make sense compared to other similar companies such as American Tower (AMT), plus the reduction in leverage is encouraging. At the end of the day, I believe that the data reinforces my ‘buy’ rating on the stock.

Staying the course

The only new data that we have had come out for Crown Castle covers the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company was $1.06 billion. That represented a decline of 4.8% compared to the $1.11 billion the business reported one year earlier. While this doesn't look good, it's not unexpected. In fact, this has been a trend for the company ever since its largest tenant, T-Mobile US (TMUS), which is responsible for 35% of its revenue today, absorbed Sprint back in April 2020. In order to achieve greater operating efficiencies, T-Mobile USA has been cutting out redundancies and inefficiencies. And that includes cancelling some of its contracts with Crown Castle.

In fact, year over year, site rental revenue for the company contracted $58 million because of non-renewals for towers. $51 million of that fell under the ‘Sprint Cancellations’ category. There were some other declines as well. For instance, the company saw a $38 million impact associated with straight line accounting changes. And it also saw a $16 million impact from a change in prepaid rent amortization that involved upfront payments received from long-term tenants and some other deferred credits. Having said that, the company had some wins as well. It enjoyed tenant additions and renewals or extensions of tenant contracts that positively affected revenue by $28 million. It also benefited from contractual cash escalations on existing tenants that pushed revenue up by $24 million.

Because of the drop in revenue and the other changes that the company has seen, such as the aforementioned asset sales, it did see a reduction in net income from $311 million last year to negative $464 million this year. This was because of losses from the disposal of discontinued operations amounting to $830 million. This stemmed from switching its Fiber Business from a continuing operation to a discontinued operation.

More representative of the company's overall health would be its cash flow figures. But here, too, we have a generally negative picture. The one exception was operating cash flow, which jumped from $599 million to $641 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $805 million to $759 million. FFO, or funds from operations, contracted from $478 million to $451 million. On an adjusted basis, it dropped from $484 million to $479 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business fell from $754 million to $722 million.

Investors would be wise to assume that this downward trend will continue for at least this year. For instance, management expects site rental revenues in 2025 to be between $3.99 billion and $4.03 billion. Even at the high end, that would be down from the $4.27 billion reported last year. It's also expected that EBITDA will fall from $3.04 billion to between $2.78 billion and $2.81 billion. Meanwhile, adjusted FFO is expected to come in at between $1.77 billion and $1.82 billion. That would represent a reduction from the $1.98 billion reported for 2024. This guidance is unchanged from what it was earlier in the year.

Once again, this kind of weakness has been expected for a while now. And the primary driver of it remains the Sprint Cancellations that the company has to contend with. That impact to site rental revenue should be around $205 million. There are some other weak spots as well, such as changes in straight line adjustments, and changes in amortization of prepaid rent. But at the same time, the company is expected to see overall organic site rental billings grow. Core leasing activity, which involves new tenants and additional investments by existing tenants, should add between $105 million and $115 million to the company's top line. Lease escalations should add another $90 million to $100 million.

If we take management's guidance as a given and if we assume that other profitability metrics will fall at the same rate, we would get FFO of about $2.17 billion and adjusted operating cash flow of $2.32 billion. However, we also need to take into consideration management's expectation for the future. The firm still believes, as of the end of the first quarter of this year, that adjusted FFO will grow to between $2.27 billion and $2.42 billion by the time its transactions are consummated.

Revenue growth, interest expense changes from ongoing operations, and savings associated with selling, general, and administrative costs achieved by these transactions, should push adjusted FFO up by between $250 million and $370 million on an annualized basis. The other $210 million to $260 million of cash flow improvements should be thanks to interest expense reductions caused by the $6 billion in debt repayment that the company is planning. In the chart above, we can see what this implies for the company for the future.

With these estimates, and with the historical results that the company achieved last year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These are not attractive multiples. Or at least, they aren't for most companies. The good news is that, if management's estimates are accurate when it comes to the post-sale condition of the company, then the stock looks valued similarly, and in some respects even more cheaply, than competitor American Tower. This is certainly encouraging to see.

Another thing to take into consideration here is the impact on leverage that investors should see. If we ignore the extra $2.5 billion in cash proceeds that the company will be receiving from its asset sales, and just assume that the money in question will go toward the $3 billion and share buybacks like management has promised, then the overall net leverage ratio of the business should be around 5.87 in the future. That's comparable to what it was using data from 2024, and it's below the 6.33 that we get with American Tower right now.

Another positive to take into consideration here is that this is an industry that continues to grow. Even during times of recession, as indicated in the image below, the company has experienced site rental revenue growth. This stability, combined with the strong cash flows that the company generates, justify a more lenient assessment of the company than if it were just some other average firm.

Even though I was not a fan of the terms that Crown Castle received for its most recent asset sales, the good news is that, in theory, the company should have a good opportunity to do well moving forward. Certainly, industry data supports this view. In the image below, you can see data provided by competitor American Tower. Between 2007 and 2024, mobile data usage grew at an annualized rate of 53% in the US. This has been spurred on by the rise of the smartphone, other connected devices, and more recently, 4G and 5G expansion.

In the years to come, further growth is practically guaranteed. By 2024, 5G mobile deployments had occurred so quickly that there was a 70% estimated 5G device market share. Furthermore, it's believed that the number of mobile connected devices by the year 2029 will hit 959 million in the US. That's up from the 697 million reported for 2024. Most of this growth will come from IoT devices.

Naturally, the growth in the number of devices should cause monthly traffic to grow as well. With devices becoming capable of handling far greater data loads, and with users utilizing them more now than they ever have before, it's believed that total monthly mobile data traffic in the US will hit 20.1 EB by the year 2029. That is up massively from the 8.5 EB estimated for 2024. Certainly, this will cause increased demand for telecommunications capabilities. And right at the center of that will be companies like Crown Castle and American Tower.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I must admit that I remain a fan of Crown Castle. Although some of my optimism has been dashed by the terms that it received for selling off some rather important assets, there is no denying that the company will be on better footing with the reduction in debt and the prospect of higher cash flows. It is now, in some ways, even better than rival American Tower, which I also have a ‘buy’ rating on. Given these factors, I believe that maintaining it as a ‘buy’ candidate makes sense.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - June U.S. NFP, Global PMIs And German Employment
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Week in review: Israel-Iran Ceasefire and upbeat market sentiment (but?)

Markets had been enjoying from renewed positive sentiment after the US intervened in the Israel-Iran conflict, which led to the reaching of a ceasefire agreement. The news sent Equities booming higher after a choppy past two weeks.

The Nasdaq reached new all-time highs on Wednesday, and the S&P 500 joined its tech-focused brother just on Thursday. Both indices had been pursuing their bullish impulses before this early afternoon's change in mood.

Donald Trump just announced that he is cancelling trade talks with Canada and the resumption of trade sanctions on Iran, sending stock markets back down to re-test their previous all-time highs.

Local highs for the Nasdaq (CFD) are at 22,632 and for the S&P 500 (CFD) 6,195.

For the rest, the week had been fairly calm despite the release of a fall in the US GDP and higher Core PCE this morning, putting stagflation talks back on the table.

Markets are also waiting for the release of the US Bank Stress Test results, releasing at 4:30 PM which may be a major market mover in the case of a bad surprise - a good moment to remind that March 2023 had seen major market turmoil as the Silicon Valley Bank failed, which led to the Federal Reserve imposing new regulations.

The Week ahead: June NFP, Global PMIs, German Employment and National Holidays

Next week could bring renewed surprises to financial markets, with traders closely eyeing Thursday’s highly anticipated Non-Farm Payrolls (NFP) report.

Typically released on the last Friday of each month, this month’s data will come a day early due to the U.S. Independence Day holiday on July 4th when markets will be closed.

Canada will also be observing its national holiday on July 1st, adding to what could be a week of lighter liquidity and potentially sharper moves around key data.

Asia Pacific Markets

Next week is going to be fairly light in terms of economic data release for Asia-Pacific countries.

China will see the release of its monthly PMI releases on Sunday evening, with the most important datapoint being China's Manufacturing PMI, with the previous data releasing in the contraction territory (below 50).

For the rest, Japan will see the most action on Tuesday with Japanese Consumer Confidence with a consensus of 33.6 at 1:00 A.M. ET and later, BoJ Governor Ueda will later appear in the annual Central Bank conference in Sintra, Portugal – Speech expected at 9:30 A.M the same day.

Australia will see the release of its Balance of Trade on Wednesday 21:30 ET, which tends to move the AUD.

Economic Data from Europe, UK and North America – US NFP, PMIs and NA Holidays

The beginning of this week's release will focus on individual Inflation monthly data for Italy and Germany on Monday, June 30, followed by the Flash CPI release for the Eurozone the following day at 5:00 A.M.

For Euro traders, also monitor the Unemployment Rate on Tuesday at 3:55 A.M. (Eastern Time).

In North America, markets await for the US Manufacturing PMI on Tuesday at 8:30 (exp at 48.8), and JOLTS at 10:00 A.M. which only moves markets on major surprises.

However, most traders will be on the sidelines to prepare for Thursday, largely the most consequential day of the upcoming week.

Non-Farm Payrolls data for June, expected at 129K released at 8:30 on the July 3rd, followed by the more market-moving Services PMI at 10:00 (exp at 50.3)

US Dollar performance versus other Forex Major counterparts

The US Dollar had started the week on a higher note as markets gapped all around with the US Intervention in the news, before giving back all its strength as sentiment got much better after the Ceasefire agreement.

Since, the Dollar has been on a flash sale. The DXY is closing below its previous swing lows.

Weekly Asset Performance

Oil is by far the worst performer, giving up most of its war premium, giving back above 11%. You can access our latest analysis on the energy commodity here.

The best performers on the week, however, are US Stocks, which finished the week off their highs as Donald Trump shook the markets again – they are still on an impressive run.

data.title: SDVY: An Outperforming Dividend ETF In The Small- And Mid-Cap Space
data.description:
The First Trust SMID Cap Rising Dividend Achievers ETF (NASDAQ:SDVY) offers a refined approach toward dividends. It looks beyond yields by adding dividend growth as a core component of its investment strategy, resulting in a fund with a profile that combines relatively modest yields compared to dividend-focused funds with a strong growth component. SDVY is a fund that is certainly worth keeping on the watchlist as one of the top performers in the category. However, potential investors should be aware of its relatively high volatility and a heavy tilt toward smaller capitalization companies. These are key factors to take into consideration at this moment amid a mixed environment for small caps, including softening growth, but attractive valuation compared to the rest of the market.

ETF Description & Highlights

SDVY provides exposure to U.S. small- and mid-cap companies that have demonstrated consistent dividend growth over recent years. The fund follows a rules-based approach, tracking the Nasdaq US Small Mid Cap Rising Dividend Achievers Index.

This index first screens companies that have increased dividends paid over the trailing twelve months compared to three and five past years. But it does not look only at dividends. Eligible companies must have generated earnings growth over the past three years, the cash-to-debt ratio should be higher than 25%, and the payout ratio below 65%. After passing this first step, companies are assigned a score combining dollar dividend growth over the past five years, higher dividend yield, and lower payout. The purpose of this score is to select the 100 companies best ranked for inclusion in the index. These companies are then equally weighted and rebalanced on a quarterly basis.

As of June 19, 2025, SDVY had $7.9 billion in assets invested in a portfolio of 189 companies, with an average market cap of roughly $7.14 billion. Its allocation is concentrated in small- and mid-caps, as expected, which represent 49.2% and 34.1% of the fund, respectively. For comparison purposes, there is a market-cap weighted ETF, the iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH), which, despite the name only mentioning mid-caps, also includes a meaningful exposure to small caps, accounting for 68.1% of the fund, while mid-caps make up 34.1%. While both have some similarities in terms of market cap exposure, SDVY has a greater bias toward smaller capitalization companies, with 15.6% of the fund classified as micro caps, and an average market cap that is lower than IJH’s $8.60 billion.

SDVY has a very diluted allocation profile, with its top ten holdings only representing 11% of the fund, and its top holding, InterDigital, weighing 1.2% of the fund. This reflects the fund's equally weighted structure, with slight allocation gaps among constituents being a function of short-term price moves before its quarterly rebalances. Besides InterDigital, other core holdings include Woodward (1.12%), Perdoceo (1.11%), Comfort Systems (1.11%), Cal-Maine (1.1%), Ralph Lauren (1.07%), Paycom (1.06%), CF Industries (1.01%), Unum (1.01%), and Axis Capital (1.0%), spanning sectors like technology, industrials, consumer staples, and financial services.

The table below presents a list of six dividend-oriented ETFs that invest primarily in small- and mid-caps and will serve as the peer group for SDVY in this analysis. The first is the WisdomTree U.S. MidCap Dividend Fund ETF (DON), the largest of the group with over $3 billion in assets under management, following a fundamentally weighted strategy, focused on mid-sized companies that pay regular dividends. Next, the WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Dividend Fund ETF (DES) also focuses on dividend yields with large exposure to small capitalization companies. Meanwhile, the ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrats ETF (REGL) emphasizes dividend consistency, favoring companies with at least 15 consecutive years of dividend increases. The ALPS O’Shares U.S. Small-Cap Quality Dividend ETF Shares (OUSM) adds quality metrics to the dividend orientation, while the ProShares Russell 2000 Dividend Growers ETF (SMDV) is a subset of the Russell 2000 index and filters small caps with at least 10 years of dividend growth. Completing the list, the WisdomTree U.S. SmallCap Quality Dividend Growth Fund ETF (DGRS) incorporates quality and growth screens to structure its portfolio.

SDVY’s largest sector allocation by far is to financial services, with 32.4% of total equities, followed by industrials at 24.5% and consumer discretionary at 13.6%. It still has a smaller allocation to consumer staples at 7.2%, technology at 6.6%, basic materials at 6.4%, energy at 4.9%, healthcare at 2.3%, and communication services at 2.1%. The fund has no exposure to real estate or utilities. It is worth noting SDVY’s heavy overweight position in financial services (+15.6%), an above-average yield sector, relative to the benchmark. Meanwhile, it is underweight in the typically low-yield technology sector (–7.9%), but its lower exposure to healthcare (–6.4%) is a bit surprising at first glance, given the relatively high yield in the sector.

If we look at the fund’s portfolio in its largest sectors, we can see that its allocation to financial services is led by banks such as Huntington Bancshares and East West Bancorp, which represent nearly half of the sector exposure. Additional holdings are primarily in the insurance (e.g., Lincoln National and Equitable Holdings), mortgage (Radian Group), and asset management industries in a diversified allocation across the sector.

Industrials is also SDVY’s core sector with most holdings in machinery (e.g., Donaldson, Graco, and ITT) and building products (e.g., Comfort Systems and Tecnoglass), making up roughly 15% of the fund. Remaining positions are spread across construction & engineering, electrical equipment, professional services, and IT services. This makes the fund relatively well-diversified, but still more concentrated in machinery and building products compared to the IJH, its market-cap weighted benchmark.

The fund has an allocation to consumer discretionary in line with the benchmark. Larger exposure is seen in household durables such as Meritage Homes and KB Home. Holdings like Tapestry and Ralph Lauren add exposure to the apparel industry, while Perdoceo and Graham Holdings represent the consumer services segment.

Beyond the top three sectors, SDVY maintains positions in growth sectors such as technology with holdings in software makers InterDigital and Clear Secure, as well as names like Universal Display and Dolby Laboratories. Meanwhile, the fund’s yield focus is reflected in still relevant holdings in a defensive sector like consumer staples through retailers PriceSmart and Andersons, while Cal-Maine Foods and Lancaster Colony add exposure to the food industry.

SDVY’s correlation with peers from a sector exposure perspective has a few key discrepancies. The fund's heavy allocation to financial services (32.4%) is the highest in the group, with most peers, except REGL and SMDV, allocating below 26% to this sector. SDVY’s overweight allocation to industrials (+5.7%) also draws attention, while DON and DES have a more conservative stance to this sector, with nearly 14% exposure. On the flip side, peers maintain significant positions in utilities and real estate, driven by their high yields, while SDVY steers clear of these two sectors. That makes SDVY’s sector correlation sit under 0.90 for most funds, except DGRS, and a weak individual stock overlay below 15% for most funds (DES, REGL, SMDV, and DGRS), which is not bad for investors looking for stock diversification.

High Dividend Growth, Lower Yield

SDVY presents the lowest dividend yield within the peer group, while funds like DES and SMDV yield nearly 3.0%, two funds with a large stance on utilities and real estate, while also sharing a heavy allocation to small caps. On the flip side, SDVY exhibits by far the highest dividend growth profile over the past 5 and 10 years, despite lagging the group over the past year, underscored by the fact that growth plays a key role in its stock selection methodology.

Attractive Valuation, In Line With Peers

SDVY trades at a P/E of 13.4x, representing an 18.7% discount to the benchmark’s 16.4x. The fund value tilt is primarily driven by its heavy allocation to financial services, a typical value sector, but also due to its underweight exposure to technology, where the P/E average is at 20x. Individual stock selection is also a key factor here. SDVY’s valuation discount is in the range of 10% to 20% in most sectors. However, the largest discount occurs in technology, consumer discretionary, and staples, where SDVY’s portfolio P/E is around 40% lower than the benchmark. The fund’s value profile is consistent with its strict stock selection methodology with regard to fundamentals, including cash-to-debt and payout ratio thresholds.

The fund’s growth profile is not limited to dividends; it includes historical earnings growth of 11.3% versus the benchmark’s 10.3%. On top of that, long-term earnings growth of 7.9% is also higher than the 3.9% for the benchmark. Technology, consumer staples, and financial services are key positive contributors at the sector level, more than offsetting slower growth in consumer discretionary and healthcare. However, sales growth lags the benchmark, reflecting SDVY’s underweight stance in the growth-oriented technology sector.

On profitability, SDVY’s EBITDA margin of 23.3% is slightly lower than the benchmark’s 23.7%. This highlights a mixed picture across sectors, with higher margins in technology and financial services not being enough to compensate for weaker numbers in healthcare and energy. On the bright side, ROE is much more favorable for SDVY, averaging 22.2% compared to 16.5% for the benchmark. Operational performance in technology’s holdings is strong at 48.5%, handily surpassing the benchmark, while most sectors show better ROE in the range of 2% to 7%. In summary, while SDVY’s above-average growth profile is consistent with earnings growth measures incorporated in its stock selection methodology, profitability is not the fund’s core factor per se, but is expected to remain at acceptable levels, underscored by a portfolio with companies that have been able to increase dividends over time.

SDVY’s valuation is slightly below the peer group average. Funds like DES (P/E 12.6x) and DGRS (11.6x) trade at low multiples, driven by their low exposure to technology. Meanwhile, SDVY has a more balanced valuation profile, highlighting its overweight exposure to value areas like financial services and a stock-picking methodology that favors higher-growth companies. SDVY’s growth orientation is also illustrated in its historical earnings and sales growth that outpace all peers.

Strong Returns, High Volatility

Dividend funds have struggled to compete with the broader market. The iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH), the benchmark for small- and mid-caps in this article, has done better than the peer group of dividend funds, with 3- and 5-year annualized returns of 12.7% and 12.8%, respectively. SDVY comes as an exception in the scenario. Its annualized total returns of 15.7% (3-year) and 16.6% (5-year) have been well ahead of the benchmark. This places SDVY as the best performing fund among peers, outpacing the second and third best funds (OUSM and DON) by more than 2% on an annual basis. This overall outperformance does not mean SDVY did not have setbacks, though. In 2024, for example, it lagged the benchmark, partially eclipsing strong gains in the prior year.

This kind of price action points to a relatively high volatility, which is confirmed by SDVY’s beta of 1.22 and 1.10 over the past three and five years, nearly 5% to 10% above the benchmark, and well above peers, which have seen beta usually under 1.0. SDVY’s capture ratios have been above 100, confirming a tendency to amplify the overall market moves, while other dividend funds have not been able to capture full market upside moves, but proved more resilient during downsides, with some funds showing participation over 100% during market declines.

In summary, SDVY has shown a distinct characteristic compared to dividend-oriented funds. Its above-average volatility is a bit surprising for a dividend fund, but is consistent with the fund’s growth profile, particularly in technology, where most of its holdings have a beta over 1.20.

SDVY’s average daily volume of $53 million is well above peers, who trade under $8 million a day. This leads to low spreads of just 0.05% compared to peers averaging 0.11%. On the other hand, its turnover ratio of 64.0% is the highest in the group, likely driven by its quarterly rebalances, which in turn are reflected in a high expense ratio of 0.59% for a passively managed fund. A weak spot for SDVY and most peers is their tracking error above 10% in most cases. The exception is OUSM, the only fund under 10%, while SDVY shows the worst numbers in the range of 13% to 15%. On risk management, SDVY’s 3- and 5-year Sharpe ratios of 0.35 and 0.69 are the best in the group, alongside OUSM, driven by the fund’s outperformance, offsetting its above-average volatility.

Final Thoughts

SDVY not only delivers on its mandate to offer a portfolio that maximizes dividend growth. It ends up combining strong earnings growth and disciplined valuation. But that comes with a caveat, a somewhat elevated volatility that is not commonly seen in dividend-oriented funds, driven by holdings in growth stocks.

With strong returns over the past years, SDVY positions itself as a fund capable of outperforming the small and mid-cap overall stock market, while offering above-average dividends, though certainly not as high as typical dividend-focused investment vehicles.

On the flip side, the big question is about what lies ahead for smaller capitalization companies. If the outlook remains complex with signs of earnings deceleration, small- and mid-caps are becoming more attractive from a valuation perspective. In this context, investor appetite is likely to favor quality and earnings resilience.

I see it as a positive for dividend funds in general and for SDVY, as it also skews toward dividend resilience and operational performance, given its above-average ROE. However, investors looking for defensiveness may not find SDVY as the best option among dividend funds, given its relatively high volatility and less-than-stellar dividend yields. Thus, I rate SDVY as a hold for value-oriented investors, but see this fund as a good opportunity to outperform the small- and mid-cap dividend funds in the long run.

data.title: Chegg: Activity And Users Plummet (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
To say that the rise of generative AI this year has upended the stock markets would not be an understatement. Beyond tariffs and the macro, the next-largest item on investors' minds that has been moving the markets the year is AI: and while that has been a positive catalyst for many tech stocks, it has also put survival into question for many companies.

Chegg (NYSE:CHGG), in my view, is one of the stocks that is most at risk. The learning and test-prep company, once ubiquitous and popular on college campuses, has seen its share price drop ~25% since the start of the year and ~50% over the past year. If that sounds harsh, it's unfortunately backed by the reality of Chegg's metrics: the company's revenue and user base are declining at similar amounts, and unfortunately, there are few meaningful catalysts that can reverse that decline.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Chegg in January, when the stock was still trading at ~$1.50 per share. Chegg has climbed dramatically from its post-tariff lows and saved itself from delisting: but at the same time, unfortunately, its core metrics have gotten worse. I am downgrading the stock to a sell.

One of the primary reasons that I originally thought Chegg had limited further downside after a sharp fall over the past year was the notion that Chegg would still be able to retain a smaller, but loyal set of users who would be willing to pay the company's monthly subscription fees. This thesis seemed reasonable up until Chegg released disastrous Q1 results, showcasing a deep decline in question activity on the company's apps and a sharp drop-off in paid users. Now, there's little doubt that ChatGPT (even the free, unpaid version, which is tremendously powerful on its own) is essentially making Chegg obsolete.

There are a couple of balls that Chegg has up in the air that are keeping some investors hopeful. The first is content licensing. Over the years, Chegg has built up a valuable "test bank" of question and answer pairs that it has successfully monetized via licensing deals, earning $4 million of revenue in Q1 (or a small ~3% slice of overall revenue).

The addition of licensing revenue streams, plus the ongoing subscription revenue (though declining) from the company's core Study Pack product, is what purportedly has some private equity parties interested in (as shown in the snapshot above, Chegg has cited that it is open to explore "strategic alternatives"). That being said, we find it unlikely that any buyer looking for a multi-year hold on this company will find Chegg's sharp subscriber declines worth rescuing.

All in all, despite Chegg's valiant efforts to stay alive, it is destined to go the way of most technologies: relegated to obsolescence as something better takes its place. The impact of ChatGPT on Chegg's business is clearly evident in the company's results, and I no longer think the company can avoid its eventual dissolution.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Chegg's latest quarterly results in greater detail, which, unfortunately, in my view, accelerated the company's troubles. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Chegg's revenue declined -30% y/y to $121.4 million, beating Wall Street's more modest expectations of $114.6 million (-34% y/y) but decelerating sharply from a -24% y/y decline in Q4 (which, in turn, also decelerated from -13% decline in Q3).

To me, the more telling evidence of Chegg's decline lies in its subscriber metrics. As shown in the chart below, the number of students who pay for Chegg Study Pack declined by 423k sequentially. The company lost 327k payers in the U.S. and 96k overseas, versus "only" a loss of 195k in Q4. The number of overall subscribers declined -31% y/y, to 3.19 million users.

Total retention rates, meanwhile, began to slip even further. Previously, in Q2 and Q3 of last year, despite subscriber declines, retention rates on a monthly basis were actually slightly positive. Since then, however, Q1 showcased a -99bps reduction in retention rates to 81.2%.

Perhaps the most telling negative indicator, however, is the fact that the number of questions asked on the Chegg platform declined -73% y/y to 2.4 million. That is a blatant illustration of the impact that ChatGPT and AI have had on Chegg's usefulness. Chegg's base of knowledge is limited: but ChatGPT's is broad. And though Chegg is also advertising its own ChatGPT-like experience built on generation AI foundations within its site, it doesn't appear to give the company any staying power at all.

Chegg's profitability is also sliding. Adjusted EBITDA cut down by more than half to just $19 million, representing a 16% margin that is 11 points worse y/y. Free cash flow declined by -36% y/y to $16 million.

To be fair, the company is taking aggressive actions to combat its declining profitability. The company is closing more offices and shedding more headcount, per CEO Nathan Schultz's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

As a result, we are once again taking proactive measures to align costs with our business outlook. We executed two restructurings in 2024, and today we are announcing further cost reduction plans. This restructuring will include expense reductions across our business, including closing physical offices in the US and Canada by the end of the year, limiting our upper funnel marketing, reducing new product development efforts, and finally cutting our general and administrative expenses."

Unfortunately, however, cost reductions will hardly be sufficient to combat huge subscriber declines and a massive decline in question activity that is a clear signal of Chegg's eventual obsolescence.

Key takeaways

I no longer have any confidence in Chegg's ability to survive in an AI-dominated world. The company's huge declines in subscribers and Q&A activity on the platform are clear signals that the company has little to offer when ChatGPT is so freely accessible, and younger students who are becoming AI-versed and AI-native will think of Chegg as a very antiquated solution. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Macquarie Healthcare Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Global healthcare stocks rose during 1Q25, outperforming the broader equity market.

Within the Fund

For 1Q25, Macquarie Healthcare Fund (MUTF:DLHIX) Institutional Class shares underperformed the Fund's benchmark, the Russell 3000 Healthcare Index.

The small/mid-cap medical products sector contributed the most to relative performance, driven primarily by stock selection as the sector trailed the broader healthcare Index. The Fund's overweight position in Halozyme Therapeutics Inc. (HALO) was the leading contributor to relative performance within the sector, as the company posted strong earnings and raised guidance. Shares of Intra-Cellular Therapies Inc. also contributed positively to performance after Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) announced it was acquiring the company. The leading detractor from relative performance in the sector was the Fund's position in Illumina Inc. (ILMN) Shares declined after Chinese regulators added the company to the unreliable entity list, putting about 7% of sales at risk.

An underweight allocation to pharmaceutical contract research and healthcare information technology contributed to relative performance as the sector underperformed the broader market. Healthcare services also contributed positively as shares in CVS Health Corp. (CVS) rallied on higher-than-expected proposed Medicare Advantage rates for 2026 and better-than-expected earnings results. Out-of-benchmark investments also contributed positively, as shares in Federal National Mortgage Association (OTCQB:FNMA) and Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corp. (OTCQB:FMCC) rose on anticipation that the Trump administration might reprivatize the entities.

The blue-chip medical products sector detracted somewhat from relative performance mainly due to stock selection. Overweight positions in large pharma companies including Roche Holding AG (OTCQX:RHHBY), Sanofi SA (SNY), AstraZeneca PLC (AZN), and Gilead Sciences Inc. (GILD) contributed positively as investors rotated into more defensive names. On the negative side, the Fund's holding in UCB SA (OTCPK:UCBJF) detracted from performance as the stock consolidated after a strong run in 2024 despite continued robust prescription trends for its anti-inflammatory drug BIMZELX. The Fund's lack of investment in Abbott Laboratories (ABT) and the underweight position in Johnson & Johnson also detracted from performance as the companies posted strong quarterly sales.

Overall, the biotechnology sector was the largest detractor from performance in the Fund due to its overweight position. The sector underperformed the broader healthcare industry as investors considered the effect changes at the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) would have on the pace of drug approvals. Also, given the rise in economic uncertainty, investors rotated into more defensive companies. The Fund's holdings in Viking Therapeutics Inc. (VKTX) and UniQure NV (QURE) were the largest detractors from performance within the sector. Viking shares declined as investors grew more cautious about obesity drugs in the current regulatory environment. UniQure shares dropped back after more than doubling in 4Q24 on positive regulatory developments for its promising gene therapy treatment for Huntington's Disease. Shares of Regeneron Pharmaceuticals Inc. (REGN) also underperformed as the company faced more competition for its retinal disease treatment Eylea. On the positive side, Alkermes PLC (ALKS) shares contributed positively to performance as the company posted better-than-expected revenues and raised guidance.

Outlook

For global healthcare investors, there are risks that short-term legislative and judicial action could overshadow the positive long-term fundamentals of the healthcare sector and of specific companies. However, we continue to see the potential for long-term opportunities within the global healthcare asset class. The baby-boom generation in the US is aging, implying expanding demand for healthcare products and services for decades to come. At the same time, middle classes in countries with emerging economies (notably India and China) are growing rapidly, creating appetites for Western-style medicine. We remain positive on the healthcare sector and its potential growth opportunities.

We believe that healthcare remains one of the few secular growth sectors in the economy. We continue to see many attractive opportunities, particularly in the biotechnology sector, where we see the potential for continued innovation in cancer treatments and gene therapy technology. We remain overweight in the biotechnology sector, particularly in small- to mid-cap companies, where we see potential for technological breakthroughs.

We continue to put a premium on disciplined, intensive research when analyzing investment opportunities for the Fund. We favor companies that exhibit such traits as proven competitiveness, seasoned management teams, and stock valuations that are discounted meaningfully from our estimates of intrinsic value. These characteristics are part of our daily considerations as we follow our conservative, stock-by-stock approach to portfolio management.

data.title: Macquarie Growth And Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Despite ascending to a new all-time high in mid-February, the broad market S&P Index was down for 1Q25, falling -4.3%, breaking a streak of five quarterly gains. Investors' apparent enthusiasm for the Trump administration's plans for lower taxes and deregulation gave way to broader concerns about the administration's escalating tariff threats and their potential to degrade economic growth. Falling consumer confidence and expectations for higher inflation also appeared to weigh on investor sentiment. In addition, stocks that had been big beneficiaries of the artificial intelligence (AI)-related investing theme came under pressure as the need for huge sums of capital expenditures for AI by US tech companies was called into question. (Source: FactSet.)

Within the Fund

For 1Q25, Macquarie Growth and Income Fund (MUTF:FGIPX) Institutional class shares outperformed the Fund's benchmark, the Russell Value Index.

At the sector level, an overweight allocation to healthcare contributed to relative performance for the quarter. Healthcare was the Fund's largest sector overweight for the quarter. Within the sector, holdings in CVS Health Corp. (CVS), Gilead Sciences Inc. (GILD), and McKesson Corp. (MCK), were the key contributors. McKesson is the nation's largest pharmaceutical distributor to hospitals, retail pharmacies, and medical offices. On the earnings front, McKesson reported third-quarter fiscal 2025 earnings, showing revenue growth (to billion) and increased per-share earnings guidance of up to year-over-year growth. In addition, the company also announced the acquisition of a national eye care company, which boosts the company's overall care capabilities.

An overweight allocation to the financials sector was a key detractor from relative performance for the quarter, driven by weak stock selection. At the stock level, a key detractor was a holding in Evercore Inc. (EVR), a global investment banking advisory firm. Despite 4Q24 earnings and revenue-beating estimates, a reported increase in expenses detracted from the company's results, with total expenses increasing 14.4% year on year. Other financial holdings that detracted from Fund performance include PNC Financial Services Group Inc. (PNC) and Invesco Ltd. (IVZ)

Outlook

Uncertainty surrounding trade policy is high, particularly as we ended the quarter and edged closer to "Liberation Day," the anticipated date for implementing broad tariffs. Indeed market return dynamics look different from the last period of trade policy uncertainty. This, along with some cooling of macroeconomic data points, could add to market volatility but the range of potential outcomes presents an opportunity for actively managed strategies that can take advantage of higher dispersion in company outcomes as firms adapt to policy change with varying levels of uncertainty.
